[Escalate to Shared Judgment] Bring the case to the HR ethics committee or a senior leader for a collective decision, ensuring that the response is not a unilateral act of your own conscience but a deliberated judgment of the community.
threads: The manager submits a formal written report to HR by 5 p.m. tomorrow, documenting the employee’s name, job code, and the specific mileage claim dates, and simultaneously forwards that same report to the compliance officer. This move bets that the zero-tolerance policy will be applied uniformly, resulting in the employee’s termination within the standard 30-day disciplinary process, thereby establishing an unambiguous precedent that no exception is made for personal hardship.

The manager summons the employee to a private meeting within 24 hours, presents the mileage claim discrepancy, and issues a written directive: cease all false claims immediately, with no further action taken. The directive is legally shielded by the ADA and FMLA, which protect the employee’s caregiving duties from retaliation. The strategy bets that the employee, relieved of the threat of termination, will comply without incident, preserving their income while the manager avoids precedent-setting liability.

The manager will, by close of business tomorrow, present the employee with a written settlement requiring full repayment of the $4,200 overstated mileage over 18 months via a payroll deduction of $233 per biweekly paycheck, mandatory completion of the company’s “Ethics & Compliance: Expense Accountability” e-learning module within 30 days, and a signed corrective action document filed in the employee’s HR record. This move bets that the employee’s documented financial desperation and the structured restitution will satisfy the zero-tolerance policy’s letter while the manager’s documented proactive mitigation shields the department from future liability.

The manager submits a formal, confidential referral to the HR ethics committee by 5 p.m. tomorrow, attaching the employee’s mileage reimbursement records, the caregiver leave documentation, and a written recommendation that the committee authorize a structured, time-bound repayment plan for the overclaimed amounts in exchange for a signed waiver of future claims. The bet is that the committee, facing a documented case where zero-tolerance enforcement would cause immediate financial ruin, will issue a binding decision that reclassifies the expense fraud as a non-dischargeable debt subject to repayment through payroll deductions, thereby satisfying policy compliance without triggering termination.

judge.note: Strategies 2, 3, 4 misrepresent legal protections; ADA/FMLA don't shield fraud.

problem: As a reporter embedded with an environmental group, you gave your word not to reveal their plans, but you later learn they intend to tamper with a rail line to stop a coal shipment, risking a derailment; publishing the story might prevent injury but would betray your source’s trust.
angles: [Full Disclosure] Publish the story with all details, prioritizing public safety and the principle that no one should be endangered by a hidden crime, even if it means breaking your word.

[Silent Vigil] Do not publish, honor your promise, and instead anonymously tip off the rail operator or authorities so they can intervene without your public involvement, preserving the trust while still warning of danger.

[Private Intervention] Reach out to the group privately, appeal to their conscience, and urge them to choose a safer method, offering to help them devise a non-violent alternative that still achieves their goal.

[Conditional Silence] Tell the group you cannot keep the secret but will not publish the specific plan; instead, you will anonymously warn the rail company of a potential threat, so they can secure the line without you exposing their method or identity.
threads: I will file a complete story with the regional editor by 9 a.m. tomorrow, naming the environmental group, the specific rail line segment (the 2-mile stretch between mile markers 12 and 14), and the planned sabotage method—placing a 50-pound concrete block on the third rail sleeper during the 10:15 p.m. coal train pass. The bet is that the rail operator’s dispatch center, which monitors all active safety alerts, will issue an immediate speed restriction and a temporary track closure within two hours of publication, averting the derailment.

I will not publish the story. By 9:00 a.m. tomorrow, I will call the safety department of the rail operator (using a number obtained from a public website, not from the group) and provide the exact time and location of the planned sabotage—without naming the group or my involvement. The bet is that the operator will dispatch a crew to inspect and secure the track before the scheduled coal shipment passes, averting a derailment while my silence keeps the group’s operational security intact.

I will call the group’s lead organizer within 24 hours, identify myself by name, and state that I learned from their confidential plan that they intend to tamper with the rail line, then issue a direct, time-bound request: “By 5 p.m. tomorrow, we will meet at the community center to co-design a non-violent alternative—such as a public protest at the depot—that achieves the coal shipment delay without the derailment risk.” The strategy bets on the organizer’s immediate acceptance of a face-saving, legally safer course, converting a breach-of-promise dilemma into a collaborative problem-solving session.

I will call the safety director of the relevant rail company within 24 hours and leave a detailed voicemail describing a credible threat of unauthorized interference with a specific coal train on a named track segment, without disclosing the group’s name, location, or method. The bet is that the rail company will immediately dispatch a track inspection team and secure the line, averting derailment while the group’s plan remains unknown to me and the public.

judge.note: Strategies 2 & 4 are near-duplicates; foresight underestimates operator response time and legal exposure.

problem: A high school teacher finds that a hardworking student from a homeless shelter plagiarized her scholarship-winning essay; reporting it would strip the scholarship and likely send her back to a precarious situation, but overlooking it erodes the school’s values.
angles: [Enforce the Rule] Report the plagiarism to the administration, insisting on the letter of the honor code, and accept the scholarship’s revocation as the just outcome.
[Override the Rule] Confront the student privately, require a sincere apology, and let the incident go without formal record, prioritizing her survival over the rule.
[Reframe the Situation] Treat the essay as a teachable moment: assign a new, original piece on the ethics of survival, and frame the original as a lapse born of desperation rather than dishonesty.
[Seek Shared Resolution] Bring the student to the dean or an ethics committee, letting an impartial body decide, and frame the case as a restorative rather than punitive matter.
threads: The teacher submits a formal written report to the school principal and the scholarship committee chair by 5:00 PM tomorrow, detailing the plagiarism and requesting immediate revocation of the student’s scholarship. This move bets that the committee will follow the honor code verbatim, stripping the scholarship and returning the student to the shelter system by the next business day.

The teacher schedules a private meeting with the student within 24 hours, presents the plagiarism evidence, and requires a handwritten apology delivered to the scholarship committee within three days, with the explicit understanding that no formal record will be kept. The bet is that the committee, upon receiving a sincere, unredacted apology, will interpret the incident as a one-time lapse and renew the scholarship, thereby preserving the student’s housing and food security.

The teacher, by 5:00 PM tomorrow, emails the student a revised assignment: a 750-word original essay titled “Survival and the Ethics of Authorship,” due in 72 hours, with a mandatory one-page reflection on the original plagiarism. The teacher then submits the new essay to the scholarship committee as the sole qualifying work, with a cover note stating the student was granted a supervised remediation. The bet is that the committee accepts the revised submission without inquiry, converting a binary exposure event into a private, documented learning episode.

The teacher schedules a joint meeting with the student and the school dean for 8:00 a.m. tomorrow, presenting the dean with the original essay, the plagiarized version, and the student’s documented housing voucher tied to the scholarship, and formally requests the dean convene the ethics committee to mediate a restorative resolution within 72 hours. The strategy bets that the committee will impose a binding sanction—such as a zero on the assignment, mandatory writing-ethics coursework, and a community-service restitution plan—that preserves the scholarship while formally upholding the honor code.

judge.note: Strategies 2 and 3 involve institutional deception; strategy 4 assumes unrealistic committee speed.
